note:
就像核电站和敏感的计算机网络一样，最安全的珍稀藏书也受到所谓的“纵深防御”的保护--一系列小而重叠的措施，旨在阻止可能能够克服单一威慑的小偷。匹兹堡卡内基图书馆珍稀书籍和档案馆所在的奥利弗室，是接近柏拉图式理想的概念。格雷格·普赖尔(Greg Priore)从1992年开始担任该房间的经理，他就是这样设计的。
房间只有一个入口，只有几个人有钥匙。当任何人，无论是雇员还是赞助人，进入藏品时，普赖尔都想知道。房间白天的时间有限，所有客人都被要求签到，并将夹克和袋子等个人物品留在外面的储物柜里。房间里的活动一直受到摄像机的监视。
另外，奥利弗的房间里也有他自己。他的办公桌坐在一个可以控制房间和顾客工作的桌子的地方。当一位顾客归还一本书时，他检查它是否完好无损。特别收藏品的安全性根本不会比奥利弗房间好多少。
于是，在2017年春天，图书馆的管理层惊讶地发现，图书馆的许多藏品都不见了。这不仅仅是因为少了几件物品。这是至少一个世纪以来美国图书馆发生的最大规模的盗窃案，被盗物品的价值估计为800万美元。
经常收藏对公众开放的特殊藏品的人有两类：一类是想要特别研究一些东西的学者，另一类是只想看一些有趣的东西的人。这两个群体经常被不可捉摸的东西所吸引。在1450年至1500年之间，欧洲活字印刷之初印刷的书籍是古老的、稀有的、具有重要历史意义的书籍。简而言之，咒语是如此珍贵，而且通常是如此显眼的东西，以至于任何想要躲避侦查的小偷都不会偷走它。奥利弗房间的小偷偷了十个。
游客和研究人员都喜欢旧地图，很少有比Orbis Terrarum剧院(俗称Blaeu Atlas)的地图更令人印象深刻的了。匹兹堡卡内基图书馆的版本印刷于1644年，最初由三卷组成，其中包含276幅手绘石版画，这些版画绘制了欧洲探索时代的已知世界。276张地图全部不见了。
多年来，图书馆的许多藏品都是创始人安德鲁·卡内基(Andrew Carnegie)和他的朋友们捐赠的。但在一个值得注意的例子中，图书馆专门拨款购买了40卷爱德华·柯蒂斯(Edward Curtis)在20世纪头几十年创作的美洲原住民的照相凹版版画。这些图像美丽，具有历史价值，极其罕见。只创作了272套；2012年，佳士得以280万美元的价格售出了一套。卡内基图书馆的布景容纳了大约1500张照相凹版“印版”--插图从一本书中分离出来，然后插入书中。一位图书专家后来指出，它们都被从装订中剪下并取下，“除了散落在各处的几个不起眼的主题”。
而这仅仅是个开始。在奥利弗房间工作的人几乎偷走了所有有重大货币价值的东西，不放过任何国家、不放过任何一个世纪、不放过任何一个主题。他拿了藏书中最古老的一本，一本印刷于1473年的布道集，也是最知名的一本，艾萨克·牛顿的98年版。他偷走了亚当·斯密(Adam Smith)的第一版“国富论”(The Wealth Of Nations)、威廉·詹宁斯·布莱恩(William Jennings Bryan)写的一封信，以及伊丽莎白·卡迪·斯坦顿(Elizabeth Cady Stanton)1898年出版的回忆录“八十年和更多：回忆1815-1897”的珍本。他偷走了美国第二任总统约翰·亚当斯(John Adams)写的一本书的第一版，以及第三任总统托马斯·杰斐逊(Thomas Jefferson)签名的一本书。他偷走了1620年在伦敦印刷的乔瓦尼·博卡西奥(Giovanni Boccaccio)的第一本英文版“十日谈”，以及241年后在同一座城市印刷的乔治·艾略特(George Eliot)的“西拉斯·马纳”(Silas Marner)的第一版。从约翰·詹姆斯·奥杜邦(John James Audubon)1851年至1854年创作的北美四足动物作品中，他偷走了155幅手绘石版画中的108幅。
简而言之，他几乎拿到了他能拿到的所有东西。他这样做了将近25年而没有受到惩罚。
当图书馆发现自己是重大盗窃的受害者时，可能需要很长时间才能确定丢失了什么；检查每个搁置的项目及其页面是一个费力的过程。但是匹兹堡卡内基图书馆的稀有和古董藏品已经有了很好的记录，自从政府开始建立该机构最稀有藏品的档案馆以来。格雷格·普赖尔(Greg Priore)几年前从附近的杜肯大学(Duqune University)获得欧洲历史硕士学位，当时他在图书馆的宾夕法尼亚房间(Pennsylvania Room)工作，这是一个致力于当地历史和家谱的空间。他还在匹兹堡大学攻读图书馆学学位，重点是档案管理。无论是纸面上还是面对面，他似乎都是管理新档案馆的完美人选，并于1991年受聘监督1992年奥利弗·房间(Oliver Room)的藏品。
普赖尔给人的印象是专业但随和，是那种知道很多，但对自己的知识知之甚少的人。他身高不到6英尺，声音洪亮，留着突出的胡子，是当地一名产科医生的儿子，一生中的大部分时间都在卡内基图书馆的步行距离内度过。在他家乡城市的一所著名学府得到一份重要的工作就像做梦一样。
得到这份工作后，他与一名保护专家一起评估卡内基图书馆的珍稀古籍。此外，两名受雇提供保护建议的珍稀图书专家发现，图书馆几乎没有考虑过保护最古老的书籍。因此，工作人员封锁了窗户以控制气候，用金属书架取代了旧的木质书架，因为木质书架可以将酸浸入书籍，并升级了安全系统。1992年，这个房间正式以长期赞助人威廉·R·奥利弗(William R.Oliver)的名字重新命名。多年来，它一直是匹兹堡卡内基图书馆的明珠。讲解员带顾客参观，C-SPAN说这是宾夕法尼亚州西部的文化高点之一。学者和记者深入研究了它的档案。
2016年秋天，图书馆官员决定再次对藏品进行审计，并聘请了波尔商城艺术顾问公司(Pall Mall Art Advisors)进行评估。克里-李·杰弗里(Kerry-Lee Jeffrey)和克里斯蒂娜·斯卡武佐(Christian Savuzzo)于2017年4月3日(星期一)开始审计，以1991年的库存为指南。不到一个小时，就有麻烦了。杰弗里在寻找托马斯·麦肯尼(Thomas McKenney)和詹姆斯·霍尔(James Hall)的“北美印第安人部落史”(History Of North America)。这一里程碑式的作品包括120幅手绘石版画，这是1821年开始的一个项目的结果，当时麦肯尼试图以全色记录访问华盛顿特区与政府安排条约的美洲原住民的服装和精神实践。这套三卷本的对开本制作于1836年至1844年之间，体积大而华丽，将是任何藏品中的一个亮点。但卡内基图书馆的版本一直藏在排在最后的顶层书架上。当杰弗里发现原因时，她的胃都要掉下来了。“曾经有一本装满盘子的丰满的书，”她回忆道，“两边都屈服了。”所有那些令人惊叹的插图都被从装订中剪下了。
鉴定人发现，许多有插图或地图的无价书籍都被洗劫过。约翰·奥吉尔比(John Ogilby)的“美国”(America)是关于新大陆的最伟大的英国插图作品之一，于1671年在伦敦印刷，其中包含51张车牌和地图。他们都走了。托勒密1548年印刷的突破性的“La Geography”一本完好无损地保存了400多年，但现在所有的地图都不见了。在乔瓦尼·皮拉内西(Giovanni Piranesi)在1748年至1807年间印刷的一套18卷极其罕见的蚀刻作品中，评价员冷淡地指出，“现场检查时，这笔资产中唯一找到的部分就是装订。这些东西显然已经从装订中拿掉了，鉴定师做出了非同寻常的假设，认为它们已经从房子里拿出来了。“。仅“海盗号”的重置价值就高达60万美元。
无论在哪里，审计人员都发现了令人震惊的破坏和抢劫程度。他们向保护部门的负责人雅卡琳·米格尼亚展示了他们的结果。她也觉得不舒服。在看到一卷又一卷的历史卷变成存根后，她回到办公室哭了起来。4月7日，在鉴定人开始调查仅仅五天后，杰弗里和斯卡武佐会见了图书馆馆长玛丽·弗朗西斯·库珀(Mary Frances Cooper)和另外两名管理员，详细说明了他们已经发现--或者更确切地说，没有发现--的细节。他们的下一阶段分析将有一个更悲观的焦点：现在他们将试图确定这些藏品的价值到底下降了多大程度。4月11日，一个星期二，库珀把奥利弗房间的锁换了。格雷格·普赖尔没有拿到钥匙
虽然其他文化遗产窃贼已经不遗余力地避免引起人们对他们行为的关注-偷窃低价值的物品，销毁卡片目录条目，撕毁书盘，从页面上漂白图书馆印章-普赖尔拿走了他能找到的最好的东西，厚颜无耻地把图书馆印章留在里面，就像图书馆开始收集书籍时看到的那样。尽管采取了这种漫不经心的方式，他还是取得了惊人的成功，比记忆中任何一位内部图书窃贼都要成功。
普赖尔和他的妻子是一名儿童图书管理员，他们几乎没有奢侈的生活方式；这对夫妇住在一间简陋的公寓里，里面挤满了书。但他们有四个孩子，他们上的是私立学校：圣埃德蒙学院、埃利斯学校和杜肯大学。
所有迹象都表明，他犯罪并不是为了致富，而是像他告诉警方的那样，只是为了“漂浮”。例如，2015年秋天，普赖尔给埃利斯学校写了一封电子邮件，要求延长学费支付期限。他写道：“我正试图同时支付4个孩子的学费。”几周后，他要求Duqune官员解除对分配给他两个孩子的账户的限制，因为他已经逾期支付了学费。2016年2月，普里奥雷要求房东延期，谎称他的妻子因心脏病发作而缺勤。房租已经逾期四个月了。
普赖尔住得离匹兹堡卡内基图书馆足够近，15分钟就能步行上班。其中一条路线让他经过了卡利班书店著名的蓝色建筑，卡利班书店是该市最著名的文化景点之一。这家商店是1991年由一位名叫约翰·舒尔曼(John Schulman)的匹兹堡人创立的，他身高5英尺7英寸，身材魁梧，头发修剪得很整齐，灰色的头发稀疏，经常会有一把灰白的山羊胡子混在几天的胡子里。
舒尔曼在20世纪80年代开始了他的图书销售业务，在匹兹堡的一套公寓里工作。他善于交际，勤奋好学，在这一行多年的信誉工作中获得了那种地位。他是美国古董书商协会(ABAA)的成员，在大西洋中部分会的理事会任职。他也是地区性机构的评估师，包括匹兹堡大学、卡内基梅隆大学和宾夕法尼亚州立大学。在销售了几十年的珍稀书籍之后，他对业内大多数人都很熟悉，甚至在业内也有一些知名度：多亏了在“古董路演”上的露面，他在公共广播公司(PBS)声名鹊起。
在很大程度上，舒尔曼对待普赖尔给他带来的书籍、地图或印刷品，就像他处理从任何来源获得的稀有和古老的材料一样。他会以市场上的人能理解的方式描述一本单独的书，并根据商品的质量将其列在他的网站上。但是，有了普赖尔带来的物品，就多了一个步骤。
当图书馆购买了一本有价值或重要的书时，图书馆会使用几种不同的印章中的一种来标记它：墨水、浮雕或穿孔。这些标记表明了图书馆的名称，意在做两件事：确认合法所有者的身份，并销毁图书的价值以供转售。大多数主要的特别收藏品，如“奥利弗房间”，也会在封面内侧贴上书盘。
要卖出一本标记如此之多的书，一个典型的小偷必须撕下、切割和漂白这些证据；如果他一不小心，他会在这个过程中毁掉这本书最初有价值的大部分东西。舒尔曼找到了另一种方法来准备一本偷来的书出售。每当他从普赖尔那里拿到一本卡内基的书时，他或他的一名员工就会用他保存在商店里的材料，在书盘的底部按下一个像口红一样鲜艳的小红色印章。它宣布这本书为“从图书馆撤回”。那个标记是用来抵消其他标记的。
虽然有图书馆员和档案员从他们本应管理的藏品中偷窃的传统，但自20世纪30年代以来，还没有像舒尔曼这样享有盛誉的交易商被牵连进来。在20世纪70年代和80年代，一位行事张扬的德克萨斯州书商和ABAA前主席约翰·詹金斯(John Jenkins)通过向图书馆和收藏家出售被盗和伪造的物品赚钱。但他的大部分渎职行为仅限于德克萨斯州-任何认识詹金斯的人都不会惊讶地发现他是一个逃犯。他是一个负债累累的赌徒，为了保险金烧毁了自己的商店，他的生命于1989年结束，头部中枪(当局对这是他杀还是自杀意见不一)。
舒尔曼经常出现在各大书展上，他似乎和业内的任何书商一样坚如磐石-所有这些都使他成为普赖尔的完美屏障。这位图书管理员不能冒险直接接近经销商或收藏家，告诉他们他卖的是哪种类型的书，而且当他第一次试图出售一本书时，互联网会让他暴露出来。如果没有舒尔曼的帮助和良好的声誉，普赖尔根本不可能运作-如果没有普赖尔，舒尔曼也不可能接触到奥利弗房间的高价物品。
显然，格雷格·普赖尔在案发前六个月就知道他会被抓。2016年秋天，当图书馆管理部门讨论对奥利弗房间进行评估的可能性时-这必然会发现失踪的资产-他提出了反对意见。但他的同事们将其归因于他对自己领域内其他人的普遍顽固，一名图书管理员指出，随着时间的推移，这种顽固变得越来越明显。尽管如此，无论有没有普赖尔的批准，政府都决定继续进行评估。
普赖尔和舒尔曼谈到了这件事，书商试图通过电子邮件向供应商发送一些可能的解释，解释为什么有很多物品丢失。舒尔曼提出，有些物品可能需要维修或出借，他敦促普赖尔制作文件证明这一点。他还建议说，图书馆现已过世的前馆长曾谈论过出售奥利弗房间的一些较好的书籍，而且他可能是在普赖尔休假期间这样做的。舒尔曼提议强调“奥利弗房间是相当漏洞百出的……[而且]毫无疑问，许多不同的工作人员和访客都有多种机会在没有适当礼仪的情况下进入房间。”
普赖尔则表示，房间的防御并不完美。2017年4月18日，当图书馆管理员采访他时，他告诉馆长库珀，他确实把编目员、实习生和志愿者留在了房间里自己工作。他补充说，维修人员-特别是一些修理过屋顶的人-可以进入房间。
尽管如此，最终还是没有办法掩盖他几十年来的罪行。数以千计的车牌、地图和照片不见了；显然，这不是享受了几分钟自由进入的赞助人或工人的作品。即使是其他人偷了图书馆的东西，普赖尔也不可能不注意到丢失了这么多东西。4月份，他被停职，6月份被解雇。
匹兹堡警方于6月份开始正式调查，并于8月24日对普赖尔的家、卡利班书店和卡利班仓库执行了搜查令。警方同一天审问了普赖尔。没过多久，他就坦白了。
当警察前往卡利班仓库时，他们带来了波尔购物中心艺术顾问公司的克里斯蒂娜·斯卡武佐(Christian Scauzzo)。在其他物品中，她发现了91幅爱德华·柯蒂斯(Edward Curtis)的印刷品和7幅布莱欧地图集(Blaeu Atlas)的地图。警方还发现了舒尔曼用来表明他卖的书已从图书馆注销的印章。
比尔·克拉斯皮曾两次毕业于凯斯西储大学(Case Western Reserve University)，拥有英国文学学士和硕士学位，目前担任该大学主图书馆特别藏书部主任。尽管他热爱人文学科，但他知道是科学让凯斯·韦斯特的灯一直亮着。这就是为什么他特别伤心地交出了一本重要的科学史书籍。
2018年8月，他收到宾夕法尼亚州阿勒格尼县地区检察官办公室侦探莱尔·格雷伯(Lyle Graber)的一封电子邮件，内容是关于最近购买的一本早期现代天文学书籍。格雷伯写道：“在审查此案的证据时，似乎是在2016年，你从…卡利班图书公司购买了乔治·普尔巴奇的Theoricas中的Quaestiones。”不幸的是，这本书很可能是从卡内基图书馆被盗并出售给像您这样毫无戒心的买家的书之一。“。
舒尔曼的目录描述指出，这本书的状况“非常好，有轻微的前图书馆标记。”克拉斯皮从书架上拿出这本书，明白了舒尔曼所说的“前图书馆标记”是什么意思：前两页有几张邮票和一个来自匹兹堡卡内基图书馆的灰蓝色长方形书盘。在书盘下面有一组红色的小字，上面写着“从图书馆撤回”。克拉斯皮小心翼翼地把书包好，然后把它送回宾夕法尼亚州。
大约在同一时间，一位名叫迈克尔·基塞尔的私人收藏家也收到了一封警告信。基塞尔买了一件普赖尔偷来的珍宝，舒尔曼把它卖给了英国一位有声望的商人。那个商人要求基塞尔把书还给格雷伯侦探，基塞尔照做了。
那年8月，数十人-私人收藏家、图书馆员和珍稀书商-收到了类似的信件。他们将这些书籍和文件送到阿勒格尼县，在那里，它们已经成为一个小型但极其有价值的图书馆的一部分，由地区检察官监督。
今年1月，在阿勒格尼县一家法院，普里奥雷承认盗窃和收受赃物，而舒尔曼承认收受赃物、欺骗和伪造盗窃。这类罪行的指导方针建议9至16个月监禁的标准刑期，但包括另外两种可能性：最高可达25个月监禁的加重刑期，以及可能包括缓刑在内的减轻刑期。
财产犯罪的量刑在很大程度上取决于数字。Pall Mall的艺术顾问们花了几个月的时间来确定之前销毁或直接被盗的每一件物品的重置价值。他们得出结论，总金额超过800万美元。但即使是这个数字，t。

note_en:
Like nuclear power plants  and sensitive computer networks, the safest rare book collections are protected by what is known as “defense in depth”—a series of small, overlapping measures designed to thwart a thief who might be able to overcome a single deterrent. The Oliver Room, home to the  Carnegie Library of Pittsburgh’s rare books and archives, was something close to the platonic ideal of this concept. Greg Priore, manager of the room starting in 1992, designed it that way.
    The room has a single point of entry, and only a few people had keys to it. When anyone, employee or patron, entered the collection, Priore wanted to know. The room had limited daytime hours, and all guests were required to sign in and leave personal items, like jackets and bags, in a locker outside. Activity in the room was under constant camera surveillance.
  In addition, the Oliver Room had Priore himself. His desk sat at a spot that commanded the room and the table where patrons worked. When a patron returned a book, he checked that it was still intact. Security for special collections simply does not get much better than that of the Oliver Room.
  In the spring of 2017, then, the library’s administration was surprised to find out that many of the room’s holdings were gone. It wasn’t just that a few items were missing. It was the most extensive theft from an American library in at least a century, the value of the stolen objects estimated to be $8 million.
    There are two types of people who frequent special collections that are open to the public: scholars who want to study something in particular, and others who just want to see something interesting. Both groups are often drawn to incunables. Books printed at the dawn of European movable type, between 1450 and 1500, incunables are old, rare and historically important. In short, an incunable is so valued and usually such a prominent holding that any thief who wanted to avoid detection would  not steal one. The Oliver Room thief stole ten.
  Visitors and researchers alike love old maps, and few are more impressive than those in  Theatrum Orbis Terrarum, commonly known as the Blaeu Atlas. The Carnegie Library of Pittsburgh’s version, printed in 1644, originally comprised three volumes containing 276 hand-colored lithographs that mapped the known world in the age of European exploration. All 276 maps were missing.
  Many of the library’s holdings had been donated over the years by the founder, Andrew Carnegie, and his friends. But in one notable instance, the library allotted money specifically to purchase 40 volumes of photogravure prints of Native Americans created by Edward Curtis in the first decades of the 20th century. The images were beautiful, historically valuable and extremely rare. Only 272 sets were created; in 2012, Christie’s sold one set for $2.8 million. The Carnegie Library’s set held some 1,500 photogravure “plates”—illustrations made apart from a book and inserted into it. They had all been cut and removed from their bindings, “except a few scattered throughout of unremarkable subjects,” a book expert later noted.
      And this was just the beginning. The person who worked over the Oliver Room stole nearly everything of significant monetary value, sparing no country or century or subject. He took the oldest book in the collection, a collection of sermons printed in 1473, and also the most recognizable book, a first edition of Isaac Newton’s  98. He stole a first edition of  The Wealth of Nations by Adam Smith, a letter written by William Jennings Bryan and a rare copy of Elizabeth Cady Stanton’s 1898 memoir,  Eighty Years and More: Reminiscences 1815-1897. He stole a first edition of a book written by the nation’s second president, John Adams, as well as a book signed by the third, Thomas Jefferson. He stole the first English edition of Giovanni Boccaccio’s  Decameron, printed in London in 1620, and the first edition of George Eliot’s  Silas Marner, printed in the same city 241 years later. From John James Audubon’s 1851-54  Quadrupeds of North America, he stole 108 of the 155 hand-colored lithographs.
  In short, he took nearly everything he could get his hands on. And he did it with impunity for close to 25 years.
    When a library finds that it has been the victim of a major theft, it can take a long time to determine what is missing; an inspection of each shelved item and its pages is a laborious process. But the Carnegie Library of Pittsburgh’s rare and antiquarian collection had already been well documented, ever since the administration moved to establish an archive of the institution’s rarest holdings. Greg Priore, who had graduated with an M.A. in European history a few years earlier from nearby Duquesne University, was then working in the library’s Pennsylvania Room, a space dedicated to local history and genealogy. He was also pursuing a library science degree at the University of Pittsburgh, with an emphasis on archives management. Both on paper and in person, he seemed like the perfect candidate to run the new archive, and was hired in 1991 to oversee what became the Oliver Room collection in 1992.
  Priore comes across as professional but easygoing, the sort of guy who knows a lot but wears his knowledge lightly. Just under six feet tall, with a resonant voice and a prominent mustache, he was the son of a local obstetrician and spent the bulk of his life within walking distance of the Carnegie Library. An important job at a prestigious institution in his home city was something like a dream.
    After getting the job, he worked alongside a preservation specialist to assess the Carnegie Library’s rare and antiquarian books. In addition, two rare book experts hired to offer conservation advice found that the library had given little thought to preserving its oldest books. So the staff blocked off windows to get control of the climate, substituted metal shelves for the old ones made of wood, which can leach acid into books, and upgraded the security system. In 1992, the room was officially renamed for William R. Oliver, a longtime benefactor. For years it served as the jewel of the Carnegie Library of Pittsburgh. Docents took patrons on tours, and C-SPAN said it was one of western Pennsylvania’s cultural high points. Scholars and journalists plumbed its archives.
  In the fall of 2016, library officials decided it was time to audit the collection again, and hired the  Pall Mall Art Advisors to do the appraisal. Kerry-Lee Jeffrey and Christiana Scavuzzo began their audit on April 3, 2017, a Monday, using the 1991 inventory as a guide. Within an hour, there was trouble. Jeffrey was looking for Thomas McKenney and James Hall’s  History of the Indian Tribes of North America. This landmark work included 120 hand-colored lithographs, the result of a project that began in 1821 with McKenney’s attempt to document in full color the dress and spiritual practices of Native Americans who had visited Washington, D.C. to arrange treaties with the government. The three-volume set of folios, produced between 1836 and 1844, is large and gorgeous and would be a highlight in any collection. But the Carnegie Library’s version had been hidden on a top shelf at the end of a row. When Jeffrey discovered why, her stomach dropped. “Once a plump book filled with plates,” she would recall, “the sides had caved in on themselves.” All those stunning illustrations had been cut from the binding.
      The appraisers discovered that many of the invaluable books with illustrations or maps had been ransacked. John Ogilby’s  America—one of the greatest illustrated English works about the New World, printed in London in 1671—had contained 51 plates and maps. They were gone. A copy of Ptolemy’s groundbreaking  La Geographia, printed in 1548, had survived intact for over 400 years, but now all of its maps were missing. Of an 18-volume set of Giovanni Piranesi’s extremely rare etchings, printed between 1748 and 1807, the assessors noted dryly, “The only part of this asset located during on-site inspection was its bindings. The contents have evidently been removed from the bindings and the appraiser is taking the extraordinary assumption that they have been removed from the premises.” The replacement value for the Piranesis alone was $600,000.
  Everywhere they looked, the auditors found a staggering degree of destruction and looting. They showed their results to the head of the Preservation Department, Jacalyn Mignogna. She, too, felt sick. After seeing historic volume after historic volume reduced to stubs, she went back to her office and wept. On April 7, only five days after the appraisers had begun their inquiry, Jeffrey and Scavuzzo met with the library’s director, Mary Frances Cooper, and two other administrators, and detailed what they had already found—or, rather, not found. The next phase of their analysis would have a more pessimistic focus: Now they would try to determine just how far the collection’s value had fallen. On April 11, a Tuesday, Cooper had the lock to the Oliver Room changed. Greg Priore was not given a key.
    Just about the only thing that keeps an insider from stealing from special collections is conscience. Security measures may thwart outside thieves, but if someone wants to steal from the collection he stewards, there is little to stop him. Getting books and maps and lithographs out the door is not much harder than simply taking them from the shelves.
  While other cultural heritage thieves have gone to great lengths to avoid calling attention to their acts—stealing items of low value, destroying card catalog entries, ripping out bookplates, bleaching library stamps from pages—Priore took the best stuff he could find, and brazenly left the library stamps in, as the library would see when it began to regather the books. Despite this cavalier approach, he was astonishingly successful, more successful than any insider book thief in memory.
  Priore and his wife, who worked as a children’s librarian, hardly had an opulent lifestyle; the couple lived in a modest apartment crowded with books. But they had four children, who attended private schools: St. Edmund’s Academy, the Ellis School and Duquesne University.
  All indications suggest that he was perpetrating his crimes not to get rich but rather, as he told police, merely to stay “afloat.” For example, in the fall of 2015, Priore wrote an email to the Ellis School asking for an extension on tuition payments. “I am trying to juggle tuition payments for 4 kids,” he wrote. A few weeks later, he asked Duquesne officials to lift a hold on accounts assigned to two of his children, since he had made overdue tuition payments. In February 2016, Priore asked his landlord for an extension, falsely claiming his wife had missed work because of a heart attack. The rent was four months past due.
    Priore lived close enough to the Carnegie Library of Pittsburgh that he could walk to work in 15 minutes. One route took him past the famous blue edifice of the  Caliban Book Shop, one of the city’s best-known cultural spots. The store was founded in 1991 by a Pittsburgher named John Schulman, who is 5 feet 7 inches tall and stocky, with close-trimmed, thinning gray hair and, often, a graying goatee blending into a few days’ growth of beard.
    Schulman started his bookselling business in the 1980s, working out of a Pittsburgh apartment. Gregarious and diligent, he acquired the sort of status that comes from years of reputable work in the profession. He was a member of the  Antiquarian Booksellers’ Association of America (ABAA), serving on its board of governors for the Mid-Atlantic chapter. He was also an appraiser for regional institutions, including the University of Pittsburgh, Carnegie Mellon University and Penn State. After decades selling rare books, he was familiar to most in the business and even somewhat well known outside of it: Thanks to appearances on “Antiques Roadshow,” he was PBS-famous.
  For the most part, Schulman treated the books, maps or prints that Priore brought him exactly as he would process the rare and antiquarian materials he got from any source. He would describe an individual book in ways people in the market would understand and, depending on the quality of an item, list it on his website. But with the items Priore brought, there was an added step.
    When a book of value or importance is acquired by a library, the institution marks it using one of several different kinds of stamp: ink, emboss or perforation. These marks, which state the name of the library, are meant to do two things: identify the rightful owner and destroy the value of the book for resale. Most major special collections, like the Oliver Room, also adhere a bookplate to the inside front cover.
  To sell such a heavily marked book, a typical thief would have to tear, cut and bleach away this evidence; if he was not careful, he would destroy in the process much of what made the book valuable in the first place. Schulman found another way to ready a stolen book for sale. Using materials he kept at his store, whenever he got a Carnegie book from Priore, he or one of his employees pressed a small red stamp, bright as lipstick, on the bottom of the bookplate. It pronounced the book “Withdrawn from Library.” That mark was to counteract the others.
  While there is a tradition of librarians and archivists stealing from collections they are meant to steward, not since the 1930s had a dealer as highly reputed as Schulman been implicated. In the 1970s and ’80s, a flamboyant Texas bookman and one-time president of the ABAA named John Jenkins made money selling stolen and forged items to libraries and collectors. But most of his malfeasance was confined to Texas—and no one who knew Jenkins would have been surprised to discover he was an outlaw. He was an indebted gambler who had burned his own store for insurance money, and his life ended in 1989 with a gunshot to the head (authorities differ over whether it was a homicide or a suicide).
      Schulman, a constant presence at the major book fairs, seemed as rock-solid as any bookseller in the business—all of which made him the perfect fence for Priore. The librarian could not risk approaching dealers or collectors directly with the types of books he was selling, and the internet would have exposed him the first time he tried to sell an incunable. Priore simply could not have operated without Schulman’s help and good name—and Schulman could not have gained access to the Oliver Room’s big-ticket items without Priore.
    Apparently Greg Priore knew he was about to get caught six months before it happened. In the fall of 2016, when the library administration was discussing the possibility of an appraisal of the Oliver Room—which would necessarily uncover missing assets—he argued against it. But his colleagues chalked it up to his general obstinacy against having others in his domain, an obstinacy that one librarian noted had grown increasingly pronounced as the years ticked by. Still, with or without Priore’s approval, the administration decided to go ahead with the assessment.
  Priore talked to Schulman about it, and the bookseller tried to help his supplier by emailing a number of possible explanations for why many items were missing. Some items might be out for repair or loan, Schulman offered, urging Priore to create documents attesting to this. He also suggested saying the library’s former director, now dead, had talked about selling off some of the Oliver Room’s better books, and that he might have done so while Priore was away on leave. And Schulman proposed emphasizing “that the Oliver Room is fairly porous and accessible...[and] that there have no doubt been multiple opportunities for many different staff and visitors to enter the rooms without proper protocol.”
  For his part, Priore suggested that the room’s defenses weren’t perfect. When library administrators interviewed him on April 18, 2017, he told Cooper, the director, that he did leave catalogers, interns and volunteers to work by themselves in the room. He added that maintenance workers—in particular, some men who had done repairs to the roof—had access to the room.
  In the end, though, there was no way to hide his decades of crimes. Thousands of plates, maps and photographs were missing; clearly, this was not the work of a patron or workman who had enjoyed a few minutes’ unfettered access. Even if someone else had been stealing from the library, it would have been impossible for Priore not to notice so much was missing. In April he was suspended from his job and in June he was fired.
  Pittsburgh police began a formal investigation in June, and on August 24 executed search warrants at Priore’s home, the Caliban Book Shop and a Caliban warehouse. Police questioned Priore the same day. It didn’t take long for him to come clean.
  When police went to the Caliban warehouse, they brought along Christiana Scavuzzo of Pall Mall Art Advisors. She found, among other items, 91 of the Edward Curtis prints and seven maps from the Blaeu Atlas. Police also found the stamp that Schulman used to indicate that the books he sold had been deaccessioned from the library.
    Bill Claspy is twice a graduate of Case Western Reserve University, with a B.A. and an M.A. in English literature, and today serves as head of special collections for the university’s main library. Despite his love for the humanities, he knows it is the sciences that keep the lights on at Case Western. That’s why he was especially sad to surrender an important book of scientific history.
  In August 2018, he received an email from Lyle Graber, a detective in the Allegheny County District Attorney’s office in Pennsylvania, about a recently purchased book of early modern astronomy. “While reviewing evidence in this case,” Graber wrote, “it appears that in 2016 you purchased  Quaestiones in Theoricas in Georgii Purbachii from...Caliban Books. Unfortunately, it is very possible that this book is among those having been stolen from the Carnegie Library and sold to unsuspecting buyers such as yourself.”
  Schulman’s catalog description noted the book’s condition was “very good with minor ex-library marks.” Claspy retrieved the book from its shelf and saw what Schulman meant by “ex-library marks”: The first two pages had several stamps and an ash-blue rectangular bookplate from the Carnegie Library of Pittsburgh. Below the bookplate was a small set of red letters pronouncing the book “Withdrawn from Library.” Claspy carefully wrapped the book and sent it back to Pennsylvania.
    Around the same time, a private collector named Michael Kiesel also received an alarming letter. Kiesel had purchased one of the incunables that Priore had stolen and Schulman had sold to a reputable dealer in England. That dealer asked Kiesel to return the book to Detective Graber, which Kiesel did.
  Dozens of people—private collectors, librarians and rare book dealers—received similar letters that August. They sent the books and documents to Allegheny County, where they have become part of a small but extremely valuable library under the supervision of the district attorney.
        This past January in an Allegheny County court, Priore pleaded guilty to theft and receiving stolen property, while Schulman pleaded guilty to receiving stolen property, theft by deception and forgery. The guidelines for such crimes recommend a standard sentence of nine to 16 months’ incarceration but include two other possibilities: an aggravated range of up to 25 months’ incarceration, and a mitigated range that could include probation.
  Much of what governs sentencing in property crimes comes down to the numbers. The Pall Mall Art Advisors spent months determining the replacement value for each item Priore had destroyed or stolen outright. The total, they concluded, was more than $8 million. But even this number, t

title: 是谁犯下了长达25年、价值800万美元的图书馆抢劫案？
title_en: Who Committed the 25-Year, $8 Million Library Heist?

note:
在我们在全国主要无线网络进行速度测试的第11个年头，我们驱车前往美国26个城市，看看5G是否辜负了炒作。答案是：还没有。虽然Verizon在我们的第一次全国性4G和5G测试中重新获得了最快移动网络的称号，但运营商的覆盖范围仍然很小。AT&amp；T&T；和T-Mobile的5G网络对它们的整体表现没有太大帮助。
自2010年以来，我们一直在对移动网络进行测试和评级，这是我们见过的最奇怪的网络过渡。首先，我们正处于一场全球大流行之中。今年，我们让20多名当地司机在我们的监督下使用三星Galaxy S10和S20系列手机测试自己的城市，而不是让少数司机沿着东海岸或西海岸一次测试多个城市。我们之所以选择这些手机，是因为它们提供了最好的4G和5G性能，S20支持美国运营商必须提供的所有不同类型的5G。
我们为每个测试城市提供单独的结果，这样您就可以看到您居住的地方哪一家航空公司最快。点击上面的地图或查看本页底部的列表，查看您的家乡或离您最近的城市。
如你所见，威瑞森在今年与美国电话电报公司(AT&amp；T)势均力敌的竞争中夺冠。在我们测试的26个城市中，威瑞森赢得了其中的13个，美国电话电报公司(AT&amp；T)夺得了12个，而T-Mobile则夺得了一个。好消息是，与去年相比，所有运营商的网速普遍都有所提高，尽管这在很大程度上是4G性能改善的结果，而不是5G的出现。
我们承认，我们相信了5G的炒作。运营商、手机制造商和芯片制造商都在销售与4G一样快、更强大、延迟更低的5G。因此，我震惊地发现，我们AT&amp；T的5G结果尤其慢于同一网络上的4G结果。
这是一场营销危机，而不是业绩危机。与我们2019年的测试相比，所有三家美国运营商都显示出明显更高的下载速度和更好的宽带可靠性。只是这些收益，尤其是AT&amp；T的收益，很大程度上是因为4G网络的改进，而不是5G网络的改进。
好消息是，几乎没有例外，你不需要急于去买一部5G手机。我们将在每个单独的城市页面上突出显示我们认为购买5G设备最明智的地方。
今年的成绩是有先例的。回顾2012年最快的移动网络，我们发现，当时的运营商MetroPCS安装的4G网络在很多地方都比AT&amp；T；的3G网络慢，原因与AT&amp；T&T；现在的5G网络速度较慢的原因相同。“Gs”不是魔术。新一代运营商只是允许运营商使用比以前更多的频谱-但如果他们没有可用的频谱，他们就不会获得那样的性能。美国电话电报公司(AT&amp；T&39；)的低频段5G目前使用的频道太窄而无关紧要，就像MetroPCS&T；的4G LTE那样。
当我们第一次开始测试时，我们调查了六家运营商：AT&amp；T、Cricket、MetroPCS、Sprint、T-Mobile和Verizon。现在，随着Sprint品牌在8月份退役，全国有三家运营商：美国电话电报公司(AT&amp；T)、T-Mobile和威瑞森(Verizon)。Cricket和MetroPCS的名字分别作为美国电话电报公司(AT&amp；T)和T-Mobile的预付费服务继续存在。美国移动电话覆盖了许多州的大部分农村地区，但它在我们测试的足够多的主要城市都没有提供服务，这是我们研究的一部分。
到目前为止，T-Mobile对Sprint的吸收并没有给消费者带来太多好处。事实恰恰相反：在我们的结果中，Sprint用户涌入T-Mobile网络似乎造成了一些拥塞，导致T-Mobile的相对表现落后。从2019年到2020年，所有运营商的网速都有所提高，但T-Mobile的网速增幅低于美国电话电报公司(AT&amp；T；)和威瑞森电信(Verizon)。
T-Mobile恳求大家耐心等待，称一旦它能够适当地将Sprint的频谱重新用于5G，性能将会提高。这一承诺似乎在几个城市开花结果，但T-Mobile必须加快行动，才能实现这一承诺。
美国运营商推出5G已经一年多了。AT&amp；T声称在我们的26个测试城市中有22个城市有5G；Verizon在18个城市有5G；T-Mobile在所有城市都有5G。但我们的5G结果在各个方面都令人失望，在每一家运营商上都是如此。通常，这是在人造G(我们稍后解释这一点)和没有G之间的一个选择。
AT&amp；T、T-Mobile和Verizon对5G采取了截然不同的方式。长话短说，美国电话电报公司(AT&amp；T5G)目前的5G似乎基本上一文不值。T-Mobile 5G比4G有很大的提升，但它的速度只是我们对一个好的4G网络的期望-它并不是一种新的体验。Verizon&#39；的5G通常令人兴奋，但很难找到。
AT&amp；T&39；的5G从旧的850 MHz蜂窝频段中剥离了一小部分，并将其分配给5G，从而在不提高性能的情况下为手机提供了有效的5G图标。而且，由于目前5G手机的工作方式，它往往会降低性能。在同时使用4G和5G的地点，我们的5G手机在22个城市中有21个比我们的4G手机速度慢。
美国电话电报公司(AT&amp；T&39；)的4G网络依赖于从广泛的频率范围内组装几个不同的4G频道。最新的手机最多可以组装7部--这就是所谓的7运营商聚合，这也是美国电话电报公司(AT&amp；T)去年获胜的原因。美国电话电报公司(AT&amp；T&39；)今年的4G表现继续出色，而4G正是该运营商赢得了近一半城市的原因。
但是，5G手机不能为一个5G频道添加那么多的4G频道。因此，如果他们处于5G模式，他们就会放弃4G信道，这样他们就可以使用非常窄的、通常是5 MHz的5G信道，结果是性能降低：对于AT&Amp；来说，使用5G手机进行测试通常比我们只使用4G的手机倒退了一步。
AT&amp；T还表示，它在35个城市拥有快速的5G；高频网络。我已经在达拉斯、拉斯维加斯和纽约测试过了。不幸的是，美国电话电报公司(AT&amp；T)没有提供该网络的地图，我们的司机似乎从未遇到过它，尽管他们去过我以前测试过的地方。
T-Mobile使用的低频段5G信道比美国电话电报公司(AT&amp；T)稍宽一些。在19个测试城市的相同地点，T-Mobile的5G网速高于4G网速，其中7个城市的5G网速低于4G网速。T-Mobile也开始使用中频5G，这可能是最佳选择。在中频城市费城，T-Mobile的5G比4G快122%。在另一个中波段城市华盛顿，它比4G快330%。T-Mobile在7个城市也有高频5G网络，但我们没有看到任何刚刚高呼出高频5G的结果。
然而，T-Mobile的4G速度今年受到了影响，这就是为什么它只赢得了一个城市的冠军。我对此有一些看法。T-Mobile正在整合大量的Sprint客户，这些客户已经获准接入T-Mobile的网络。我们以前也见过网络因拥塞而变慢；最著名的是，2011年Verizon收购iPhone时就发生了这种情况。在大多数地方，T-Mobile正在体面地处理涌入其网络的新客户，但洪水可能会让它有点倒退。
T-Mobile表示，最终，它的客户将受益，因为它清理了Sprint的中频4G频谱，并将其全部重新用于中频5G。情况很可能是这样，我们从费城和华盛顿看到的结果是有希望的。但就目前而言，它在过渡阶段有点挣扎。
值得一提的是，美国电话电报公司(AT&amp；T；)和T-Mobile的5G网络都比不上加拿大的贝尔(Bell)和Telus的4G网络。贝尔和Telus能够在没有一点5G的情况下超过我们的5G技术。
所以你不禁要问，美国电话电报公司(AT&amp；T)和T-Mobile除了渴望拥有5G网络之外，为什么还要为此烦恼呢？营销是一件大事，但当他们告诉我5G是未来的时候，我相信他们的工程师。尽管运营商还没有足够的频谱来让5G发挥作用，但当他们安装新的网络硬件时，它是完全支持5G的，所以他们最好还是把它打开。
当美国市场上仍然充斥着4G设备时，我在这篇文章中谈到了很多关于5G的内容。但我认为，无论你是否真的在寻求一部5G手机，5G的采用率在明年都会大幅上升。目前，三星的所有旗舰手机都是5G手机，我们很可能会在今年10月看到5G iPhone问世。5G手机也变得越来越便宜，T-Mobile的新款Revvl 5G手机售价不到400美元。
运营商围绕这项新技术做出了很多承诺，其中一些可能会在即将到来的一年里实现。你可以在我们关于什么是5G的专题中阅读这些承诺？自2018年末首次承诺5G家庭互联网以来，我们一直在等待5G家庭互联网开始弥合数字鸿沟。Verizon在少数几个城市的非常有限的地区提供了这项服务，但我们发誓，到今年年底，我们会看到更多的行动。5G家庭互联网也是T-Mobile在收购Sprint时向政府承诺的一部分，尽管我们还没有看到这一点。
除此之外，还有一些潜在的新应用，比如移动网络如何通过3G改变了我们的生活，社交媒体如何通过4G改变了我们的生活。早在2017年的一次黑客马拉松上，我看到了5G如何帮助父母与NICU中的婴儿联系。OnePlus的首席执行官若有所思地说，5G可能会让本地存储过时；高通总裁说，这可能意味着你再也不用升级游戏机了。使用5G(和6G)作为一种质量可控的工业超级Wi-Fi，我已经在韩国的T.um看到了关于未来全息通信和超精密机器人手术的演示。高通的XR2耳机和Spatial软件可以让你参加逼真的增强现实社交或商务会议，而且有了5G，你不需要靠近路由器就能做到这一点。
Verizon提供了唯一一种提供与4G真正不同体验的5G形式，但几乎没有任何一种形式可用。Verizon的毫米波5G显示速度高达2Gbps，延迟远低于10ms。这可以在未来实现全新的增强和虚拟现实应用程序。但我们没有记录Verizon 5G在任何城市超过11%的地点，可用性更多的时候是下降到2%到3%。
毫米波使用非常脆弱的短程面板，很容易被障碍物阻挡。在我们的测试中，毫米波通常不能穿透建筑物，甚至玻璃也有问题；我们让司机把车窗调低，手机朝外，所以网络甚至有机会。
到目前为止，还没有一家运营商的5G实现了延迟承诺。LAG不适用于多人游戏或虚拟现实，也不适用于网真和远程手术等新应用。使用5G作为具有高服务质量的受保护超级Wi-Fi的想法在一定程度上依赖于低延迟。
我们的大部分测试是在5G网络上进行的，这些网络在初始握手时使用4G，所以不要比4G有更好的延迟。但我们看到了一个新世界的一瞥。T-Mobile在巴尔的摩、波士顿、纽约、普罗维登斯和华盛顿的网络显示出10-15ms的延迟。这比我们从4G上看到的都要好。
但等等：Verizon在堪萨斯城和圣地亚哥的5G延迟在10ms以下，其他几个城市的延迟在10ms到11ms之间。根据高通的说法，一旦你低于7ms左右，VR应用程序就开始变魔术了。这有助于支持我们的观点，即到目前为止，Verizon的5G是唯一真正具有变革性的5G。
所有这一切可能会让你想知道，美国电话电报公司(AT&amp；T)和T-Mobile什么时候能获得变革性的5G速度，Verizon什么时候能获得足够的5G覆盖。Verizon计划今年晚些时候在全国范围内宣布5G，使用一种名为DSS的技术，根据请求的负载将现有的4G频道分为4G和5G。由于它不提供任何更宽的频道，这可能不会比Verizon现有的4G快太多。
运营商需要的要么是5G的新频谱，要么是将快速的高频5G扩展到更多人的方法。这已经在路上了，尽管不是马上。在我的分析中，至少需要50 MHz的专用5G频谱才能真正发挥作用。中频，在2 GHz到7 GHz之间，最有希望，因为它们可以相对容易地覆盖城市。T-Mobile已经通过购买Sprint获得了这一功能；它只需打开它。一个新的280 MHz，名为C-Band，将在今年年底拍卖-可能会在三家运营商之间平分，并于2021年推出。另一个100兆赫将在2022年年中挂牌竞标。
与此同时，Verizon正在研究扩展其高频网络的方法。该公司与Pivotal Commware达成了一项协议，后者生产可以扩大5G覆盖范围的社区中继器。未来的电话和基站芯片组还可以扩展高频5G范围。这不仅仅让Verizon受益。虽然Verizon在其高频段5G方面达成了最大的交易，但三大运营商都在全国范围内持有高频段。
因为5G和新冠肺炎危机这两大变化，我们2020年的驾驶与往年有很大的不同。
传统上，我们会游览每个城市，然后在进入下一个城市之前测试城市之间的农村地区。但这包括机票、租车和酒店，今年我们都不觉得使用这些东西是安全的。所以我们雇了大约24名司机，每个人都在自己的车里测试自己的城市，睡在自己的床上，把测试套件从一个地方运到另一个地方。结果是一项全国性的、COVID安全的测试，但没有我们通常提供的农村数据。
5G是一个更大的变化。AT&amp；T在我们的22个测试城市提供5G服务，T-Mobile在全部26个城市提供5G服务，Verizon在16个城市提供5G服务。由于大多数美国人仍在使用4G手机，我们想看看4G和5G手机性能之间的差异，因此我们让不同的4G和5G手机运行测试，测试间隔60秒。
最初，我们预计5G手机的速度总是比4G快。我们震惊地发现，情况往往并非如此。因此，对于每个测试站点，我们最终从同一网络上的两台设备中选择最佳结果，无论它们位于哪个G上。
我们4G用的是三星Galaxy S10系列手机，5G用的是三星Galaxy S20系列手机。之所以选择S10，是因为它们拥有我们测试过的性能最好的4G调制解调器。之所以选择S20，是因为在测试时，它们是唯一可以处理5G频段的整个“分层蛋糕”-低、中、高-的手机。
我们的司机在每个城市至少12个地点停车，每站15分钟。每个位置的测试结果被平均得出该位置的总体结果。然后，所有地点的结果被平均到一个城市总分中，所有在停靠站之间收集的“移动”结果被平均到两个虚拟的“地点”中。
人们真正想要的是始终如一的宽带体验--他们不在乎手机上的图标显示了什么。所以我们的总分反映了这一点。我们使用的是具有以下变量的加权平均值：
虽然我们提到我们在排行榜上看到5G的频率有多高，但这并没有计入总分。手机上是否有4G或5G图标在技术上很有趣，但对消费者来说，真正重要的是体验，而不是技术。如果一家运营商的4G网络比另一家运营商的5G网络更快，我们不会给5G运营商积分。
尽管我们有很多发现，但我仍然相信5G将改变我们的生活方式。但它不会像美国航空公司目前推出的那样做到这一点。我们看到的网络不是“第四次工业革命”，也不是运营商本月用来推销5G的其他任何东西。
取而代之的是，我们目前的大多数5G覆盖范围都为人们提供了略有改善的4G体验，并装饰了一个闪亮的新图标。这并不坏，但为了兑现他们关于5G将如何改变教育、医疗、工业和家庭互联网的崇高承诺，运营商将需要使用比他们目前提供给我们的更多的频谱和更好的技术。
有关全国5G推广的更多信息，请在Race to 5G上查看我们的月度更新和运营商记分卡。
note_en:
For our 11th year of speed testing major wireless networks across the country, we drove to 26 US cities to see if  5G lives up to the hype. The answer: Not quite yet. While Verizon regains the title of fastest mobile network in our first nationwide 4G and 5G test, the carrier still has very little coverage. And AT&T's and T-Mobile's 5G networks don't help their overall performance much.
 We've been testing and rating mobile networks since 2010, and this is the strangest network transition we've ever seen. For one thing, we're in the midst of a global pandemic. Rather than have a fewer number of drivers work their way down the east or west coasts testing multiple cities at a time, this year we had two dozen local drivers test their own cities under our supervision using  Samsung Galaxy S10 and  S20 series phones. We chose these phones because they offer the best 4G and 5G performance available, with the S20 supporting all the different types of 5G US carriers have to offer.
 We provide individual results for each of our test cities so you can see which carrier is the fastest where you live. Click into the map above or check out the list at the bottom of this page to see your home city or the one closest to you.
   As you can see, Verizon takes the crown this year in a tight race with AT&T. Of the 26 cities we tested, Verizon won 13 of them, AT&T took 12, and T-Mobile grabbed one. The good news is that speeds are generally up across the board for all carriers compared with last year, even if that's largely the result of improved 4G performance rather than the emergence of 5G.
   We admit it, we bought into the 5G hype. Carriers, phone makers, and chip makers alike have all been selling 5G as faster and more powerful than 4G, with lower latency. So I was shocked to see that our AT&T 5G results, especially, were slower than 4G results on the same network.
 This is a crisis for marketing, not for performance. All three US carriers showed significantly higher download speeds and better broadband reliability than they did in our 2019 tests. It's just that these gains, particularly on AT&T, are largely because of improvements in 4G, not 5G networks.
 The good news is, with few exceptions, you don't need to rush out and buy a  5G phone. We'll highlight on each of our individual city pages where we think it's smartest to buy a 5G device.
   There's some precedent for this year's results. Looking back at  Fastest Mobile Networks 2012, we found the 4G installed by then-carrier MetroPCS was slower than AT&T's 3G network in many places, for the same reason that AT&T's 5G network is now slow. “Gs“ aren't magic. A new generation just lets carriers use more spectrum than they could before—but if they don't have that spectrum available, they won't get that performance. AT&T's low-band 5G is currently on channels that are too narrow to matter, like MetroPCS's 4G LTE was.
 When we first started testing, we surveyed six carriers: AT&T, Cricket, MetroPCS, Sprint, T-Mobile, and Verizon. Now, with the retirement of the Sprint brand in August, there are three nationwide carriers: AT&T, T-Mobile, and Verizon. The Cricket and MetroPCS names live on as prepaid offerings from AT&T and T-Mobile, respectively. US Cellular covers mostly rural areas in many states, but it doesn't have service in enough of the major cities we test to be part of our study.
 So far, T-Mobile's absorption of Sprint hasn't shown much advantage for consumers. It's to the contrary, really: In our results, it looks like the rush of Sprint users onto T-Mobile's network has created some congestion that has caused T-Mobile to fall behind in comparative performance. All of the carriers' speeds increased from 2019 to 2020, but T-Mobile's increased less than AT&T's and Verizon's did.
   T-Mobile pleads patience, saying that once it's able to properly reuse Sprint's spectrum for 5G, performance will improve. That promise appears to be bearing fruit in a few cities, but T-Mobile must move faster to make that real.
     It's been more than a year since the US carriers launched 5G. AT&T purports to have 5G in 22 of our 26 test cities; Verizon has it in 18; and T-Mobile has it in all of them. But our 5G results were disappointing all around, on every carrier. Often, it was a choice between faux G (we’ll explain this shortly) and no G.
  AT&T, T-Mobile, and Verizon take very different approaches to 5G. To make a long story short, AT&T 5G right now appears to be essentially worthless. T-Mobile 5G can be a big boost over 4G, but its speeds are only what we'd expect from a good 4G network—it isn't a new experience. Verizon's 5G is often mind-blowing, but very difficult to find.
  AT&T's 5G slices off a narrow bit of the old 850MHz cellular band and assigns it to 5G, to give phones a valid 5G icon without increasing performance. And because of the way current 5G phones work, it often  reduces performance. At locations with both 4G and 5G, our 5G phone was slower than our 4G phone in 21 out of 22 cities.
  AT&T's 4G network relies on assembling several different 4G channels from a wide range of frequencies. The most recent phones are able to assemble up to seven of them—that's called seven-carrier aggregation, and it's why AT&T  won last year. AT&T's 4G performance continues to be excellent this year, and that 4G is how the carrier won nearly half of our cities.
 But 5G phones can't add as many 4G channels to a 5G channel. So if they're in 5G mode, they’re giving up 4G channels so they can use that extremely narrow, often 5MHz 5G channel, and the result is slower performance: faux G. For AT&T, using a 5G phone in testing was often a step  backward from our 4G-only phone.
 AT&T also says it has a fast, "5G+" high-band network in 35 cities. I've tested it in  Dallas,  Las Vegas, and  New York. Unfortunately, AT&T doesn't offer maps of that network, and our drivers never seemed to encounter it, even though they went near locations where I've tested it before.
   T-Mobile uses a somewhat wider low-band 5G channel than AT&T does. T-Mobile's 5G speeds were faster than 4G speeds at the same locations in 19 test cities, and they were slower in seven. T-Mobile is also starting to use mid-band 5G, which might be the sweet spot. In Philadelphia, a mid-band city, T-Mobile's 5G was 122 percent faster than its 4G. In Washington, another mid-band city, it was 330 percent faster than 4G. T-Mobile also has a high-band 5G network in seven cities, but we didn't see any results that just screamed out "high-band 5G."
 However, T-Mobile's 4G speeds suffered this year, which is why it only won one city. I have some theories about this. T-Mobile is in the middle of integrating a huge number of Sprint customers, who have been given access to T-Mobile's network. We've seen networks slow down because of congestion before; most famously, it happened when Verizon got the iPhone in 2011. T-Mobile is handling the influx of new customers on its network decently, in most places, but the flood may be setting it back somewhat.
 T-Mobile says that ultimately, its customers will benefit as it clears out Sprint's mid-band 4G spectrum and reuses it all for mid-band 5G. That very well may be the case, and the results we see from Philadelphia and Washington are hopeful. But for now, it's struggling a little in the transition phase.
 It's worth mentioning that neither AT&T's nor T-Mobile's 5G networks are faster than the  Bell and Telus 4G networks in Canada. Bell and Telus were able to outpace our 5G technologies without a lick of 5G.
  So you have to ask, why are AT&T and T-Mobile even bothering, other than their desire to market having 5G? Marketing is a big deal, but I believe their engineers when they tell me that it just comes down to 5G being the future. Even though the carriers don't have enough spectrum to make a difference with 5G yet, as they install new network hardware, it's all 5G-capable, so they might as well turn it on.
 I'm talking a lot about 5G in this piece when the US market is still mainly filled with 4G devices. But I think 5G adoption is going to rise sharply over the next year, whether or not you're actually seeking a 5G phone. Already, all flagship Samsung phones are 5G, and we'll likely see 5G iPhones appear this October. 5G phones are getting cheaper, too, with T-Mobile's new Revvl 5G coming in at under $400.
 Carriers are making a lot of promises around the new technology, some of which we may see realized over the upcoming year. You can read up on those promises in our feature on  What Is 5G? We've been waiting for 5G home internet to start bridging the digital divide since that was first promised in late 2018. Verizon has that service in very limited parts of a few cities, but swears we'll see more action on that by the end of this year. 5G home internet is also part of what T-Mobile promised the government when it absorbed Sprint, though we haven't see it yet.
 Beyond that, though, there are potential new applications as radical as how the mobile web changed our lives with 3G and social media changed our lives with 4G. Way back at a hackathon in 2017, I saw how 5G  could help parents connect with infants in NICUs. The CEO of OnePlus mused that  5G may make local storage obsolete; the president of Qualcomm said it may mean you'll never again have to upgrade a game console. Using 5G (and 6G) as a sort of quality-controlled, industrial "super Wi-Fi," I've seen  demos on future holographic communication and super-precise robotic surgery at South Korea's T.um. Qualcomm's  XR2 headset and Spatial software put you in realistic, augmented reality social or business meetings, and with 5G you don't need to be right near a router to do it.
 Verizon offers the only form of 5G that delivers a genuinely different experience from 4G—but there's hardly any of it available. Verizon's millimeter-wave 5G showed speeds up to 2Gbps and latencies well under 10ms. That can enable radical new augmented and virtual reality apps in the future. But we didn't record Verizon 5G at more than 11 percent of locations in any city, and availability was more often down at two to three percent.
 Millimeter-wave uses very weak, short-range panels that are easily blocked by obstacles. In our tests, millimeter-wave doesn't generally penetrate buildings, and even has trouble with glass; we had our drivers keep their windows down, with the phones facing out, so the network even had a chance.
   No carrier's 5G is living up to latency promises yet. Lag won't work for multiplayer games or VR, or new applications like telepresence and remote surgery. The idea of using 5G as a protected super-Wi-Fi with high quality of service in part relies on low latency.
 Most of our testing was on 5G networks that use 4G for their initial handshakes, and so don't have much better latency than 4G. But we saw glimpses of a new world. T-Mobile's network in Baltimore, Boston, New York, Providence, and Washington showed latencies of 10-15ms. That's better than we've ever seen from 4G.
 But wait: Verizon's 5G in Kansas City and San Diego had latencies under 10ms, with several other cities in the 10-11ms range. According to Qualcomm, once you get under 7ms or so, magic starts to happen for VR applications. This helps support our contention that Verizon's 5G is the only truly transformative one so far.
  All of this may make you wonder when AT&T and T-Mobile will get transformative 5G speeds, and when Verizon will get plenty of 5G coverage. Verizon plans to announce nationwide 5G later this year using a technology called DSS that splits existing 4G channels into 4G and 5G based on requested load. Since it doesn't offer any wider channels, that probably won't be much faster than Verizon's existing 4G.
 What the carriers need is either new spectrum for 5G, or ways to extend the speedy high-band 5G to more people. That's on its way, although not immediately. In my analysis, it takes at least 50MHz of dedicated 5G spectrum to make a real difference. Mid-band frequencies, between 2GHz and 7GHz, have the most promise as they can cover cities relatively easily. T-Mobile already has that available through its Sprint purchase; it just has to turn it on. A fresh 280MHz, called C-Band, will be auctioned at the end of this year—that will probably be split up between the three carriers and launched in 2021. Another 100MHz will go up for grabs in mid-2022.
  Meanwhile, Verizon is working on ways to extend its high-band network. The company made a deal with Pivotal Commware, which makes neighborhood repeaters that can extend 5G coverage. Future phone and base station chipsets can also extend high-band 5G range. This won't just benefit Verizon. Although Verizon makes the biggest deal about its high-band 5G, all three major carriers have nationwide high-band holdings.
  Our driving in 2020 was very different from previous years because of two big changes: 5G and the COVID-19 crisis.
 Traditionally, we'd tour each city and then test rural areas between cities before moving on to the next one. But that involves flights, rental cars, and hotels, none of which we felt safe using this year. So we hired roughly two dozen drivers to each test their own cities, in their own cars, sleeping in their own beds, shipping the testing kits from place to place. The result is a nationwide, COVID-safe test, but without the rural data we usually provide.
   5G was an even bigger change. AT&T offers 5G in 22 of our test cities, T-Mobile has it in all 26, and Verizon has 5G in 16. With most people in the US still using 4G phones, we wanted to check out the difference between 4G and 5G performance, so we had separate sets of 4G and 5G phones running tests offset by 60 seconds from each other.
 Initially, we expected the 5G phones to always show better speeds than 4G. We were shocked to find that often wasn't the case. So for each test stop, we ended up choosing the best result from each of the two devices on the same network, no matter what G they were on.
 We used Samsung Galaxy S10 series phones for 4G and Samsung Galaxy S20 series phones for 5G. The S10s were chosen because they have the best-performing 4G modems we've tested. The S20s were chosen because at the time of testing, they were the only phones that could handle the entire “layer cake” of 5G bands—low, middle, and high.
 Our drivers stopped in at least 12 locations per city, for 15 minutes per stop. Test results at each location were averaged out into an overall result for that location. Then, results for all locations were averaged into an overall city score, with all of the “mobile” results collected between stops being averaged into two virtual “locations.”
 What people really want is a consistent broadband experience—they don't care what the icon on their phone says. So our overall scores reflect that. We used a weighted average with the following variables:
  While we mention how frequently we saw 5G on our charts, it doesn't factor into the overall score. Whether a phone has a 4G or a 5G icon on it is technically interesting, but what really matters to consumers is the experience, not the technology. If a carrier's 4G network is faster than another carrier's 5G network, we're not going to give that 5G carrier points.
  Despite many of our findings, I still believe that 5G is going to change how we live. But it isn’t going to do so the way US carriers are currently rolling it out. The networks we’re seeing aren’t a “fourth industrial revolution,” or whatever else the carriers are using to pitch 5G this month.
 Instead, most of our current 5G coverage offers people a slightly improved 4G experience dressed up with a shiny new icon. That’s not bad, but to live up to their lofty promises about how 5G will change education, medicine, industry, and home internet, the carriers will need to use more spectrum and better technology than they’re currently giving us.
 For more on the nation's 5G rollout, see our monthly updates and carrier score card over at   The Race to 5G .
title: 在美国26个城市测试运营商网络显示，虽然4G的整体速度有所提高，但5G没有达到炒作的要求，AT&T的5G比其4G慢很多
title_en: Testing carrier networks in 26 US cities shows that while 4G speeds are up overall, 5G does not live up to the hype, with AT&T's 5G largely slower than its 4G

note:
“在线自由和观点多样性法案”将修改“通信正义法”第230条，以内容决定是否客观合理为保护条件，同时也限制了平台可以采取行动的内容。
1996年的“通信体面法案”(Communications Decency Act)第230条赋予互联网公司免于因第三方在其网站上发布的内容而提起诉讼的豁免权，并允许它们做出善意的努力来限制内容。自特朗普总统今年5月发布针对该法案的行政命令以来，该法案受到了越来越多的审查。
这项法律被认为是网络公司的基础性法律，被撤销的威胁越来越多地被提出为迫使立法者，特别是右翼议员做出改变的大棒。
这项新提案只会将保护扩大到那些限制访问内容的公司，因为它有合理的理由相信这些内容属于1996年法案中指定的类别之一。
第230条的原创措辞给了平台广泛的空间来追求内容审核，针对淫秽、肮脏、过度暴力、骚扰或其他令人反感的内容。
周二提出的法案将打击原本令人反感的措辞-它允许平台删除广泛的危险内容-取而代之的是“宣扬自我伤害、宣扬恐怖主义或非法”。
该法案还将改变信息内容提供商的定义，将编辑或修改由另一个人或实体创建的内容的任何情况包括在内，而不是改变外观。
这一变化似乎表明，做事实核查内容或打击部分内容等事情的平台将不再受到第230条的保护，有效地中和了专家所说的那种严厉的内容审查，这种审查对于限制错误信息在网上的传播至关重要。
参议院司法委员会本月早些时候提出了一项这样的法案，将第230条的保护与打击儿童性虐待材料的努力联系起来。
消除滥用和猖獗忽视互动技术法案将允许联邦和州政府对托管儿童剥削内容的在线公司提出索赔。
批评人士表示，这项立法在阻止网上儿童性虐待材料方面作用不大，还会危及加密，这使得公司或政府无法访问设备之间的私人通信。
两党平台问责和消费者透明度法案仍在委员会中，该法案将要求平台在调整用户内容的决定方面有更大的透明度。
特朗普在5月份签署了一项行政命令，其中包括指示商务部内的一个机构向联邦通信委员会(Federal Communications Commission)提交请愿书，以澄清第230条的范围。
该子机构国家电信和信息管理局(National Telecications And Information Administration)今年早些时候提交了这份请愿书，最近结束了公开评议期。
这项行政命令是在推特首次在特朗普的一条推文上贴上事实核查标签几天后签署的，这条推文错误地声称邮寄投票将导致选举舞弊。
note_en:
The Online Freedom and Viewpoint Diversity Act would modify Section 230 of the Communications Decency Act by conditioning the protection on whether content decisions are "objectively reasonable," while also limiting the things platforms can act on.
 Section 230 of the 1996 Communications Decency Act, which has come under increased scrutiny since   President Trump targeted it in an executive order in May, gives internet companies immunity from lawsuits for content posted on their sites by third parties and allows them to make "good faith" efforts to moderate content.
  The law is considered foundational for online companies, and the threat of having it revoked has increasingly been proposed as a cudgel to compel platforms to make changes by lawmakers, especially ones on the right.
 This new proposal would only extend the protection to companies that restrict access to content where it has the "reasonable belief" it falls into one of the specified categories in the 1996 act.
 Section 230's original phrasing gives platforms wide latitude to pursue content moderation targeted at "obscene, lewd, lascivious, filthy, excessively violent, harassing, or otherwise objectionable" content.
 The bill introduced Tuesday would strike the "otherwise objectionable" phrase — which has allowed platforms to take down a wide range of dangerous content — and replace it with ‘‘promoting self-harm, promoting terrorism, or un-lawful."
 The bill would also change the definition of "information content provider" to include any instances of editorializing or modifying content created by another person or entity beyond changes to appearance.
  That change would seem to suggest that platforms that do things like fact-check content or strike parts of it would no longer get Section 230 protections, effectively neutering the sort of tough content moderation that experts say is crucial to limiting the spread of misinformation online.
  The Senate Judiciary Committee advanced one such bill earlier this month that would tie Section 230 protections to efforts to combat child sexual abuse material.
 The Eliminating Abusive and Rampant Neglect of Interactive Technologies Act would allow federal and state claims against online companies that host child exploitation content.
 Critics say the legislation would do little to stop child sex abuse material online and also endanger encryption, which makes it impossible for companies or governments to access private communications between devices.
  The bipartisan Platform Accountability and Consumer Transparency Act, which would require greater transparency around platforms’ decisions to moderate their users’ content, remains in committee.
  Trump signed an executive order in May that, among other things, directs an agency within the Commerce Department to file a petition with the Federal Communications Commission to clarify the scope of Section 230.
 That subagency, the National Telecommunications and Information Administration, filed that petition earlier this year and recently closed the open comment period.
 The executive order was signed just days after Twitter first appended a fact-checking label to one of Trump's tweets, which falsely claimed mail-in voting would lead to a rigged election.
title: 三名参议院共和党人提出了一项新法案，旨在缩小第230条的范围，通过更具限制性的语言修改平台的保护
title_en: Three Senate Republicans introduced a new bill aiming to narrow the scope of Section 230, modifying platforms' protections through more restrictive language

note:
在宣布合并协议近一年后，数字广告平台Tbraola和OutBrain之间的合并谈判已经结束。
两家公司在2019年10月宣布了合并，称两家公司每月的用户数量将达到20亿人，在Facebook和谷歌这两家主导数字广告生态系统的围墙花园之外，将为广告商提供更多有意义的选择。这两家公司在2019年10月宣布合并，称每月将有20亿人参与，并为广告商提供更多有意义的选择。Facebook和谷歌是数字广告生态系统的主导力量。
据一位知情人士透露，在宣布合并交易近一年后，数字广告公司Tbraola和OutBrain之间的合并谈判已经结束。
这些公司会在内容盒上放置标题，比如“8位患有重病的名人”或“我给了HelloFresh A Taste”之类的标题。这就是为什么我永远不会回头，而且还要投放传统的网络广告，为他们所在的出版商网站带来收入。合并谈判的结束最先由以色列出版物报道，并由一位熟悉交易的消息人士向CNBC证实。
两家公司在2019年10月宣布了合并，称两家公司每月的用户数量将达到20亿人，在Facebook和谷歌这两家主导数字广告生态系统的围墙花园之外，将为广告商提供更多有意义的选择。这两家公司在2019年10月宣布合并，称每月将有20亿人参与，并为广告商提供更多有意义的选择。Facebook和谷歌是数字广告生态系统的主导力量。两家公司当时表示，根据最初的交易条款，OutBrain股东将获得合并后公司30%的股份，外加2.5亿美元现金。
尽管美国司法部(US Department Of Justice)在调查后决定不对这笔交易提出质疑，但合并也因英国和以色列的反垄断审查而被推迟。
但消息人士称，随着时间的推移和今年塔博拉方面的业务增长，塔博拉试图改变交易条款，支付不到2019年条款的一半，两家公司无法达成协议。
note_en:
Nearly a year after announcing a deal to unite, merger talks between digital advertising platforms Taboola and Outbrain have ended.
 The companies announced the merger in October 2019, saying that together they would reach 2 billion people a month and give advertisers more "meaningful choice" outside of the "walled gardens" of Facebook and Google, which dominate the digital advertising ecosystem.
 Nearly  a year after announcing a deal to unite, merger talks between digital advertising companies Taboola and Outbrain have ended, according to a person familiar with the matter.
 The companies place content boxes with headlines such as "8 Celebs Who Have Severe Illnesses" or "I Gave HelloFresh A Taste. Here's Why I'm Never Going Back" and also deliver traditional web ads, pulling in revenue for the publisher websites that they sit on. The end of the merger talks were  first reported by  Israeli publications and confirmed to CNBC by a source familiar with the deal.
  The companies  announced the merger in October 2019, saying that together they would reach 2 billion people a month and give advertisers more "meaningful choice" outside of the "walled gardens" of  Facebook and  Google, which dominate the digital advertising ecosystem. In the terms of the original deal, Outbrain shareholders would have received shares representing 30% of the combined company plus $250 million in cash, the companies said at the time.
 Though the U.S. Department of Justice  decided not to challenge the deal after investigating it, the merger was also delayed by antitrust scrutiny in U.K. and in Israel.
 But with so much time passed and a bump in business on the Taboola side this year, Taboola sought to change the terms of the deal and pay less than half than the 2019 terms, and the companies couldn't reach an agreement, the source said.
title: 消息人士：数字广告平台Tbraola和OutBrain在宣布合并一年后未能就修改后的交易条款达成一致，已结束合并谈判
title_en: Source: digital advertising platforms Taboola and Outbrain have ended merger talks after failing to agree on revised deal terms, a year after announcing merger

note:
台北-《日经亚洲评论》(Nikkei Asian Review)了解到，苹果已经克服了旅行限制和其他冠状病毒干扰，于9月中旬开始5G iPhone的初步生产，将生产延迟从几个月缩小到只有几周。
制造业将开始规模有限，预计9月底至10月初将逐步开始大规模生产。两位知情人士表示，这一时间表仍然落后于苹果过去几年的通常时间表，当时苹果在8月份开始批量生产9月份发布的系列产品，但与几个月前的情况相比，这是一个很大的改善。
然而，考虑到失去的时间，苹果可能达不到今年的生产目标。两位知情人士称，这家加州科技巨头订购了多达8000万部5G iPhone的零部件，但消息人士称，今年的实际产量可能在7300万至7400万部之间，其余的推迟到2021年初。
与此同时，消息人士称，苹果还大幅增加了即将推出的iPad的生产订单，以满足远程工作和远程学习的需求，这表明该公司对今年剩余时间需求稳定的前景持乐观态度。
苹果周二宣布，将于9月9日举行年度发布会发布会。15日，预计将在那里推出新产品。
5G手机的生产将从至少一款机型开始，这是最低价的版本，配备6.1英寸OLED屏幕和两个后置摄像头。消息人士补充说，这款手机约占苹果5G手机生产订单的40%。最昂贵的机型将是6.7英寸、三摄像头的5G iPhone。
苹果最近在没有新冠肺炎太多干扰的情况下，成功地加快了新iPad的生产。其中一位知情人士说，9-12月新iPad的生产订单高达2,700万台，几乎相当于去年下半年生产的所有iPad型号(包括新款和旧款)。据日经早些时候报道，苹果已经增加了2020年上半年的iPad订单，以满足冠状病毒大流行刺激的相对健康的需求。
一位知情人士称，苹果还在准备一款名为Airtag的新配件，它将允许用户跟踪非苹果设备，并补充说，这款新设备已经投入生产。
苹果公司宣布，首批使用苹果内部设计的中央处理器(而不是英特尔的CPU)的MacBook也将在未来几个月发布，而彭博社(Bloomberg)报道称，Apple Watch和HomePod的更新版将于今年开始销售。
然而，几位知情人士表示，苹果目前没有计划在明年春天推出新一代经济型iPhone SE。一些市场观察人士曾预计，该公司每年都会修改这一模式，以重振销售。今年4月发布的iPhone SE起价为399美元，在苹果准备推出首款5G iPhone之际，它帮助提振了苹果的总出货量，并保持了销售势头。根据国际数据公司(IDC)的数据，苹果的iPhone出货量在4-6月这一季度同比增长11.2%，使其成为唯一一家在全球大流行期间销量出现增长的智能手机制造商。
苹果不遗余力地减少备受期待的5G智能手机的生产延迟。
在过去的几年里，该公司在制造地点派驻员工，以解决批量生产准备阶段的任何工程问题。然而，与冠状病毒相关的旅行限制迫使苹果公司远程完成大部分工作，并要求所有供应商向美国交付最终样本。一位消息人士称，为了保密，所有物品，即使是很小的物品，都被装在巨大的箱子里，装上专用卡车运往香港，在那里它们被装上一架飞往加州的飞机，这样那里的首席设计师和工程师就可以在样本上签字。
这位知情人士说，今年这种秘密的样品交付增加了很多，但苹果愿意花同样多的钱来保护他们高度机密的未完成产品。
另一位供应链高管表示：我们已经设法大大缩短了延误时间，我们不会就此止步。一些型号的最终组装仍将在10月初进行，但我们正在努力使生产尽可能早地进行。
今年新款iPhone的工程验证过程对苹果来说尤其具有挑战性，因为从4G连接到5G连接的复杂程序和重大规格升级涉及这家美国公司的工程师及其亚洲供应商的大量来回亲身测试。
加利福尼亚州的原地避难所命令和针对进入中国的外国人的现行检疫要求严重扰乱了5G iPhone的开发，以至于苹果在3月份甚至讨论了将5G iPhone系列的发布推迟到明年的可能性。
苹果计划推出总共四款5G iPhone，屏幕尺寸分别为5.4英寸、6.1英寸和6.7英寸，所有这些手机都将使用先进的有机发光二极管(OLED)显示技术。
据日经早些时候报道，这家美国科技巨头今年早些时候最初订购了大约1亿部iPhone的零部件，但在冠状病毒爆发后，这一目标降低到了8000万部左右。
知情人士称，与过去几年典型的制造模式不同，即将推出的5G iPhone每周的计划生产量将相似，这应该有助于供应商招聘、培训和维持生产线工人，避免劳动力短缺和成本飙升。
消息人士称，苹果的零部件供应商，以及最终装配商富士康、和硕和纬创目前都在为即将到来的iPhone的生产做准备，尽管这四款5G iPhone的开始生产日期各不相同。
生产线将逐条合格，进入批量生产模式。一位高管消息人士称，更大规模的大规模生产将在10月初左右进行。
今年，5G iPhone和新款iPad的所有生产仍在中国。据日经早些时候报道，AirPods装配商Luxshare-ICT于7月从纬创手中收购了一家iPhone工厂，明年将开始组装少量iPhone。
广发证券(GF Securities)分析师杰夫·普(Jeff Pu)表示，苹果押注于5G iPhone以推动销售增长。尽管今年是新冠肺炎年，但苹果保留了来自供应链的相对健康的订单，我们没有看到最终生产受到太多干扰。他表示，目前看来，延误看起来是可控的。
我们认为，到今年年底，苹果iPhone的销量将主要得益于所有5G机型的推出，我们预计苹果iPhone的销量将恢复增长，预计到2021年，苹果iPhone的销量将从今年的1.9亿部恢复增长，预计到2021年将达到2.15亿部。
note_en:
TAIPEI -- Apple has overcome travel curbs and other coronavirus disruptions to begin initial production of its 5G iPhones in mid-September, narrowing the production delay to just weeks instead of months, the Nikkei Asian Review has learned.
 Manufacturing will begin on a limited scale, with mass production expected to begin gradually between the end of September and early October. This timetable is still behind Apple's usual schedule over the past few years, when mass production began in August for lineups released in September, but it is a large improvement compared with the situation a few months ago, two sources familiar with the matter said.
 Given the lost time, however, Apple may fall short of its production target for the year. The California tech giant ordered components for up to 80 million 5G iPhones, but sources say the actual number produced this year may end up being between 73 million and 74 million, with the rest deferred into early 2021, two sources with knowledge of the matter said.
  At the same time, Apple has also significantly boosted manufacturing orders for the upcoming iPads to meet demand for teleworking and remote learning, sources said, an indication of the company's optimistic outlook for stable demand for the remainder of the year.
 Apple announced Tuesday that it will hold its annual launch event on Sept. 15, where it is expected to unveil new products.
 Production of the 5G phones will start with at least one model, the lowest-priced version with a 6.1-inch OLED screen and two rear cameras. This model accounts for around 40% of the production orders Apple has placed for 5G handsets, sources added. The most expensive model will be the 6.7-inch, triple-camera 5G iPhone.
 Apple managed to gear up production for new iPads recently without much disruption from COVID-19. Production orders for new iPads have reached as much as 27 million units for September-December, one of the people said, nearly as much as all iPad models -- both new and old -- produced in the second half of last year. Apple already  increased its iPad orders for the first half of 2020 to meet relatively healthy demand spurred by the coronavirus pandemic, Nikkei earlier reported.
 Apple is also preparing a new accessory, known as the Airtag, that will allow users to track non-Apple devices, a source with knowledge of the device said, adding that the new gadget has already gone into production.
 The first MacBooks using Apple's in-house designed central processing units, rather than Intel's CPUs, are also scheduled to be released in the coming months, the company announced, while Bloomberg has reported that updated versions of the Apple Watch and HomePod will go on sale this year.
 However, Apple currently has no plans to introduce a new generation of the budget iPhone SE next spring, several people with knowledge of the matter said. Some market watchers had expected the company to revamp the model each year to revive sales. The iPhone SE, released this April with a starting price tag of $399, has helped boost Apple's total shipment volume and sustain sales momentum as the company prepared for its first 5G iPhone lineup. Apple's iPhone shipments grew 11.2% on the year during the April-to-June quarter, according to IDC data, making it the only smartphone maker that saw unit sales increase amid the global pandemic.
 Apple has gone to great lengths to reduce the production delays for its much-anticipated 5G smartphones.
 In past years, the company stationed staff at manufacturing sites to resolve any engineering issues in the runup to mass production. Coronavirus-related travel restrictions, however, have forced Apple to do much of this work remotely and to request all suppliers deliver final samples to the U.S. To maintain secrecy, one source said, all items, even tiny ones, were packed into huge boxes and shipped in special-purpose trucks to Hong Kong, where they were loaded onto a plane bound for California, so lead designers and engineers there could sign off on the samples.
 "This year this kind of secretive sample delivery increases a lot, but Apple is willing to spend as much money to protect their highly-confidential unfinished products," the person said.
 "We've managed to shorten the delay significantly and we are not stopping here," said another supply chain executive. "The final assembly for some models is still going to be early October, but we are working to keep moving the production as early as possible."
 The engineering verification processes for this year's new iPhones have been particularly challenging for Apple, as the complicated procedures and major spec upgrades from 4G to 5G connectivity involve a lot of back-and-forth, hands-on testing by both the American company's engineers and its suppliers in Asia.
 The state of California's "shelter-in-place" order and the ongoing quarantine requirement for foreigners entering China disrupted development of the 5G iPhones so severely that in March Apple even discussed the possibility of  postponing the launch of the lineup until next year.
 Apple plans to roll out a total of four 5G iPhones with three different screen sizes -- 5.4-inches, 6.1-inches and 6.7-inches -- all of which will use the advanced  organic light-emitting diode, or OLED, display technology.
 The U.S. tech giant initially ordered components for around 100 million iPhones earlier this year, but  lowered that target to around 80 million after the outbreak of the coronavirus, Nikkei reported earlier.
 Unlike the typical manufacturing pattern over the past years, in which production is ramped up week by week, the planned production volume for the upcoming 5G iPhones will be similar for each week, which should help suppliers recruit, train and sustain production line workers and avoid labor shortages and spiking costs, sources familiar with the situation said.
 Apple's component and parts suppliers, as well as final assemblers Foxconn, Pegatron and Wistron, are currently gearing up in preparation for the production of the upcoming iPhones, though the start date for production for the four 5G iPhone models varies, sources said.
 "Production lines will be qualified one by one to enter mass production mode. The bigger scale of mass production will happen around the beginning of October," one executive-level source said.
 All production of the 5G iPhones and new iPads remains in China this year. AirPods assembler Luxshare-ICT, which bought an iPhone plant from Wistron in July, will  begin assembling a small number of iPhones next year, the Nikkei reported earlier.
 Jeff Pu, an analyst with GF Securities, said Apple is betting on the 5G iPhones to drive sales growth. "Even though it's the year of COVID-19, Apple is reserving relatively healthy orders from the supply chain and we don’t see much disruption for the final production. The delay looks manageable at the moment," he said.
 "We think Apple iPhone sales will be mostly boosted by the rolling out of all 5G models by the end of this year, and we expect Apple’s iPhone unit sales will return to growth, reaching an estimated 215 million units by next year of 2021, from 190 million units for this year."
